questions.specification: In biomechanics, which term describes the turning effect of a force about a joint?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.2 Fluid mechanics > B.2.2.3 Buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.2.3 Buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about Archimedes' principle and defines buoyant force as equal to the weight of displaced fluid, which is directly within buoyancy in fluid mechanics.

options.content: Torque (moment)
options.markscheme: Correct: torque (moment) is the turning effect of a force about a pivot/joint.

options.content: Impulse
options.markscheme: Incorrect: impulse relates to change in momentum (force × time), not the turning effect about a joint.

options.content: Work
options.markscheme: Incorrect: work is energy transferred (force × displacement), not rotational effect about a joint.

options.content: Power
options.markscheme: Incorrect: power is the rate of doing work, not the turning effect about a joint.

questions.specification: Which principle relates the speed of blood flow in a vessel to the pressure difference across it?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.2 Fluid mechanics > B.2.2.3 Buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.2.3 Buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about the principle linking airflow speed over a wing to pressure differences producing lift (Bernoulli’s principle), which falls under fluid mechanics—specifically buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids.

options.content: Archimedes' principle
options.markscheme: Archimedes' principle explains buoyancy (upthrust) as equal to the weight of fluid displaced; it does not relate flow speed in a vessel to pressure differences.

options.content: Bernoulli's principle
options.markscheme: Bernoulli's principle states that in a moving fluid, higher flow velocity is associated with lower pressure. In blood vessels, regions of faster blood flow tend to have lower pressure than regions with slower flow, linking flow speed to pressure difference.

options.content: Magnus effect
options.markscheme: The Magnus effect describes lift on a rotating object moving through a fluid; it specifically requires rotation and is not the general relationship between flow speed and pressure in a vessel.

options.content: Pascal's law
options.markscheme: Pascal's law states that pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally in all directions; it does not describe the inverse relationship between flow speed and pressure.

parts.content: Identify three types of motion relevant in sports performance.
parts.markscheme:

- Angular motion (rotation about an axis) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (any may be credited):

Additional acceptable points (any may be credited):

parts.content: Describe the role of angular motion in the spinning of sports equipment (for example, a ball), with reference to the soccer free kick in the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- Angular motion defined as rotation of an object about an axis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spinning equipment/ball exhibits angular motion that can influence its flight path/trajectory (e.g., via the Magnus effect) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spin can help stabilize flight via gyroscopic effects (maintains orientation/reduces wobble) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Direction/type of spin (e.g., topspin/backspin/sidespin) affects the amount/direction of curve and/or subsequent bounce behaviour <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (any may be credited):
- Identify that sidespin can produce lateral swerve in a free kick (Magnus force acts perpendicular to airflow and spin axis) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Credit other appropriate examples (tennis, cricket, golf) linked to angular motion and control <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept discussion of the Magnus effect or control in sports like tennis.</note>

parts.content: Explain how linear and angular momentum interact during a complex movement such as a backflip, with reference to the gymnast in the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- Linear momentum defined as $p=mv$ (mass $\times$ linear velocity) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Linear momentum is generated during take-off through impulse (force applied against the ground over time), contributing to upward/forward motion and time in the air <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Angular momentum is generated by torque at take-off, initiating rotation about an axis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conservation of angular momentum in flight: athlete changes body configuration (moment of inertia) to change angular velocity (tuck faster, extend slower) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (any may be credited):
- Link that insufficient linear momentum (insufficient vertical velocity) reduces flight time, making it harder to complete the required rotation even with high angular velocity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Link that insufficient angular momentum/torque at take-off leads to under-rotation even if jump height is adequate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reference that air resistance produces small external torques/forces but is usually minor compared with take-off effects for a gymnast <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 1, explain how an athlete’s understanding of fluid mechanics can influence equipment choice and technique in cycling.
parts.markscheme:
- From Table 1, the aero tuck reduces drag compared with upright (38 N to 28 N), so technique/equipment that promotes a streamlined posture reduces drag and improves performance (less resistive force to overcome) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples of equipment choices that help reduce drag: aero helmet, skin suit, deep-section wheels, smooth cable routing (any suitable example) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- From Table 1, lift is also reduced in the aero tuck (6 N to 3 N); athletes may adjust posture/positioning to manage lift as well as drag to improve stability/handling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use of drafting/positioning to reduce effective air resistance (reduced relative wind speed/pressure drag in the slipstream) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (any may be credited):
- Explain that reducing frontal surface area and/or promoting more laminar flow delays separation and can reduce pressure drag <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mention testing/feedback methods (e.g., wind tunnel, track aero testing) to optimise position and equipment choices <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Other acceptable answers include the use of wind tunnels for testing or adaptation to altitude/density conditions.</note>

parts.content: Describe buoyant force, and relate it to fluid displacement and body volume.

questions.specification: In which scenario would an athlete experience the lowest drag force, assuming similar speeds?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.2 Fluid mechanics > B.2.2.3 Buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.2.3 Buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about minimizing drag force across different sport scenarios (streamlining, turbulent flow, frontal area), which is directly about drag in fluids and how body position affects drag.

options.content: Running with a streamlined posture and minimal frontal area
options.markscheme: Correct. Drag force depends on fluid density, drag coefficient (related to streamlining), frontal area, and velocity ($F_d\propto \rho C_d A v^2$). For similar speeds, moving through air (low $\rho$) with a streamlined posture and small frontal area (low $C_d$ and $A$) gives the lowest drag.

options.content: Swimming with turbulent flow around the body
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Water has much higher density than air, greatly increasing drag for similar speeds, and turbulent flow increases $C_d$ compared with more streamlined flow.

options.content: Cycling in an upright position with a large frontal area
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Although cycling is in air, an upright position increases frontal area and typically increases $C_d$, so drag is higher than for streamlined running at similar speed.

options.content: Kayaking in water with a wide paddle
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Movement through water (high density) produces much greater drag for similar speeds; a wide paddle increases the effective area interacting with the water.

options.content: The gravitational field strength is lower.
options.markscheme: While the gravitational field strength $g$ decreases slightly with altitude (which would theoretically increase range), this effect is negligible compared to the reduction in air resistance.

options.content: The air density is lower, reducing drag.
options.markscheme: At higher altitudes, the air density $\rho$ is significantly lower. Since the drag force $F_D$ is proportional to the air density ($F_D \propto \rho$), the resistive force is greatly reduced, allowing the projectile to maintain its velocity for longer and travel a greater horizontal distance.

options.content: The concentration of oxygen increases lift.
options.markscheme: Lift is an aerodynamic force that depends on air density and flow patterns, not the concentration of specific gases like oxygen. Furthermore, air density decreases with altitude, which would decrease lift.

options.content: The buoyant force increases.
options.markscheme: The buoyant force is equal to the weight of the air displaced ($B = \rho V g$). Since the air density $\rho$ is lower at high altitudes, the buoyant force actually decreases.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.2.3 Buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Question is about why dimples make a golf ball travel farther via aerodynamic effects (drag/turbulence, boundary layer behavior), which falls under buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.2 Fluid mechanics > B.2.2.1 Projectile motion and the effects of forces on flight path
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.2.1 Projectile motion and the effects of forces on flight path
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Secondary link because the farther flight distance relates to projectile motion influenced by external forces like drag; however the core concept is fluid drag rather than projectile kinematics.

options.content: Dimples increase drag.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Although dimples can increase skin-friction drag slightly, their main aerodynamic effect is to reduce total drag by delaying flow separation.

options.content: Dimples promote a turbulent boundary layer, delaying flow separation and reducing overall drag.
options.markscheme: Correct. Dimples trip the boundary layer to turbulent, which delays separation, reduces wake size and pressure (form) drag, so the ball travels farther.

options.content: Dimples reduce pressure drag by keeping the boundary layer laminar for longer.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. A laminar boundary layer separates earlier on a sphere at golf-ball Reynolds numbers, increasing wake size and pressure drag; dimples work by promoting turbulence, not maintaining laminar flow.

options.content: Dimples reduce the ball’s weight in air, so gravity has less effect.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Dimples do not change the ball’s weight; gravity acts essentially the same. The main effect is on aerodynamic forces (drag/lift).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.2.1 Projectile motion and the effects of forces on flight path
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is about projectile release angle (>45°) and its effect on horizontal range (shorter distance due to more vertical motion), which is core projectile motion/flight path analysis.

options.content: The discus travels the maximum horizontal distance.
options.markscheme: In the standard projectile motion model, maximum horizontal distance is achieved at an angle of $45^\circ$ when launched and landing at the same height with no air resistance.

options.content: The discus travels a shorter horizontal distance due to increased vertical motion.
options.markscheme: For a fixed launch speed with no air resistance and equal release/landing heights, the range is $R=\frac{v^2}{g}\sin(2\theta)$. The maximum occurs at $\theta=45^\circ$. For $\theta>45^\circ$, $\sin(2\theta)$ decreases, so horizontal range decreases: the vertical component increases while the horizontal component decreases, giving a higher trajectory but a shorter range.

options.content: The release angle has no effect on the horizontal distance.
options.markscheme: In projectile motion, the range depends on launch angle via $\sin(2\theta)$, so changing the angle away from $45^\circ$ changes the horizontal distance.

options.content: Air resistance (drag) decreases as the release angle increases above $45^\circ$.
options.markscheme: Increasing the launch angle does not inherently decrease drag; drag depends on speed, shape, and air properties. A higher-angle throw may increase time of flight, potentially increasing the total effect of air resistance.

questions.specification: A swimmer finds it harder to move quickly in a pool than to run on land. Which principle best explains the greater resistive force when moving through water compared with air?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.2 Fluid mechanics > B.2.2.3 Buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.2.3 Buoyancy, drag, and lift effects on objects moving through fluids (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question compares moving in water vs running on land and asks which principle explains the increased difficulty; this is primarily due to higher drag force in a denser fluid, which falls under buoyancy/drag/lift effects in fluid mechanics.

options.content: Water’s higher fluid density leads to much greater drag force.
options.markscheme: For motion at typical swimming speeds, resistive (drag) force in a fluid is often modelled as $F_D=\tfrac12\rho C_D A v^2$ so, for the same body shape/area and speed, larger fluid density $\rho$ gives larger drag. Water has much higher $\rho$ than air, so drag is much greater in a pool than when moving through air.

options.content: Buoyancy increases the resistive force opposing forward motion.
options.markscheme: Buoyancy acts upward and reduces effective weight; it is not the main force opposing forward motion. The force opposing forward motion is primarily drag.

options.content: Water’s higher viscosity produces greater resistive (frictional) forces.
options.markscheme: Viscosity can contribute to resistance, but for typical swimming conditions the key comparison is that drag (often proportional to $\rho$ in the quadratic-drag model) is much larger in water than in air; viscosity alone is not the best single explanation here.

options.content: Higher hydrostatic pressure in water opposes forward motion more than in air.
options.markscheme: Hydrostatic pressure increases with depth but does not act as a net horizontal resistive force in steady forward motion; drag is the main cause of the increased resistance.
